items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.title: Impact of Alcohol on Medical Conditions and Medications
items.content: Even at low doses, alcohol can exacerbate medical problems and interfere with medications metabolized in the liver. It can mimic conditions like diabetes and depression, making it crucial for physicians to consider alcohol use in differential diagnoses.
keyPoints: Alcohol affects almost every neurochemical system in the brain.
Low doses of alcohol can worsen most medical issues.
Alcohol interferes with medications metabolized by the liver.
Higher doses may mimic conditions such as diabetes and depression.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.title: Prevalence and Economic Impact of Alcohol Use Disorder
items.content: The lifetime risk for alcohol use disorder is significant, with 20% in men and 10% in women. The economic burden on the U.S. exceeds $249 billion annually, highlighting the importance of screening and intervention.
keyPoints: Lifetime risk for alcohol use disorder: ~20% in men, ~10% in women.
U.S. yearly costs exceed $249 billion.
Drinking more than three standard drinks per day increases cancer and vascular disease risks.
Alcohol use disorders can reduce lifespan by approximately 10 years.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Ethanol Blood Levels and Standard Drink Quantities
items.content: Ethanol blood levels are measured in milligrams or grams per deciliter, with ~0.02 g/dL resulting from one standard drink. A standard drink contains approximately 10-12 g of ethanol.
keyPoints: 1 standard drink = 10–12 g of ethanol
~0.02 g/dL blood level from one typical drink
340 mL beer, 115 mL wine, and 43 mL 80-proof beverage each constitute a standard drink
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.title: Metabolic Pathways of Ethanol
items.content: Ethanol is primarily metabolized in the liver, with alcohol dehydrogenase converting it to acetaldehyde and aldehyde dehydrogenase further breaking down acetaldehyde. At high concentrations, the microsomal ethanol-oxidizing system (MEOS) also contributes.
keyPoints: Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) converts ethanol to acetaldehyde
Aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) breaks down acetaldehyde
Microsomal ethanol-oxidizing system (MEOS) responsible for ≥10% of ethanol oxidation at high concentrations
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.title: Neurotransmitter Effects and Withdrawal Phenomena
items.content: Acute alcohol consumption enhances GABA activity and inhibits NMDA receptors, while chronic use leads to receptor upregulation. Alcohol withdrawal is marked by decreased GABA activity and increased NMDA receptor activity.
keyPoints: Alcohol boosts $ \gamma $-aminobutyric acid (GABA) activity
Inhibits postsynaptic N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors acutely
Withdrawal involves decreased GABA and increased NMDA receptor activity
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.title: Understanding Alcohol's Impact on Brain Reward Circuits and Legal Intoxication Levels
items.content: Alcohol affects the ventral tegmental region by enhancing dopamine activity and interacting with cannabinol receptors, leading to changes in brain reward circuits. Legal intoxication levels vary but are generally set at 0.08 g/dL, with some states considering lowering this threshold.
keyPoints: Alcohol enhances dopamine activity in the ventral tegmental region.
Legal intoxication is typically defined as a blood alcohol concentration of 0.08 g/dL.
Behavioral changes can occur at lower levels (0.02–0.04 g/dL).
Death can occur with blood alcohol levels between 0.30 and 0.40 g/dL.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.title: Mechanisms and Consequences of Alcohol Tolerance and Withdrawal
items.content: Chronic alcohol use leads to tolerance through metabolic, cellular, and behavioral adaptations. Withdrawal symptoms can be severe shortly after cessation due to these adaptations not resolving immediately.
keyPoints: Metabolic tolerance involves up to 30% increase in hepatic ethanol metabolism after 1–2 weeks of daily drinking.
Cellular tolerance maintains normal functioning despite alcohol presence, leading to withdrawal symptoms when levels decrease.
Behavioral tolerance allows individuals to function better under the influence through learned adaptations.
Withdrawal syndrome peaks within the first 5 days and can include protracted symptoms like disturbed sleep for up to 4–6 months.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Alcohol-Induced Blackouts: Understanding Mechanisms and Thresholds
items.content: Blackouts are episodes of temporary anterograde amnesia common among drinkers, particularly prevalent in those with alcohol use disorders. En bloc blackouts occur at blood alcohol levels >0.20 mg/dL, while fragmentary blackouts occur at >0.12 mg/dL.
keyPoints: 35% of all drinkers experience a blackout; up to 50% among college students and higher in those with alcohol use disorders.
En bloc blackouts: blood alcohol levels >0.20 mg/dL.
Fragmentary blackouts: blood alcohol levels >0.12 mg/dL.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Sleep Disturbances and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: Alcohol disrupts sleep architecture, leading to reduced REM and deep sleep early in the night. It exacerbates snoring and sleep apnea, particularly in older men with alcohol use disorders.
keyPoints: Alcohol can cause disturbed sleep even after one or two drinks before bedtime.
75% of men aged ≥60 years with alcohol use disorders experience symptoms of sleep apnea.
Sleep impairments contribute to relapses in individuals with alcohol use disorders.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Neurological Consequences of Chronic Alcohol Use
items.content: Chronic high doses of alcohol can lead to peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar degeneration, and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome due to thiamine deficiency. Brain imaging may show ventricular enlargement in long-term users.
keyPoints: ~10% of individuals with alcohol use disorders develop peripheral neuropathy.
Cerebellar degeneration occurs in ~1% of those with alcohol use disorders.
Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome affects approximately 1 in 500 individuals with alcohol use disorders.
Brain ventricular enlargement and widened cortical sulci occur in ~50% of long-term users.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.title: Alcohol's Impact on Hematologic and Cancer Risks
items.content: Chronic alcohol use affects the hematopoietic system by increasing red blood cell size (MCV) due to its impact on stem cells. Heavy drinking, especially when coupled with folic acid deficiency or malnutrition, can lead to hypersegmented neutrophils, reticulocytopenia, and sideroblastic changes. Additionally, alcohol increases cancer risk; even low levels of consumption elevate the risk for various cancers.
keyPoints: Alcohol use leads to increased MCV reflecting effects on stem cells.
Heavy drinking with folic acid deficiency can cause hypersegmented neutrophils and reticulocytopenia.
1.5 drinks per day increase a woman's breast cancer risk 1.4-fold.
Four drinks per day increase the risk for oral, esophageal, and rectal cancers threefold and 1.5 times respectively.
Seven to eight or more drinks per day result in approximately fivefold increased risk for many other cancers.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.title: Alcohol's Role in Liver Disease and Pancreatitis
items.content: Chronic alcohol consumption significantly raises the risk of acute pancreatitis, with an incidence rate threefold higher than in the general population. It also contributes to various liver diseases by impairing gluconeogenesis and promoting fat accumulation in liver cells, potentially leading to cirrhosis observed in 15% of individuals with alcohol use disorders.
keyPoints: Incidence of acute pancreatitis is ~25 per 1000 per year, threefold higher in those with alcohol use disorders.
Alcohol impairs gluconeogenesis and increases fat accumulation in liver cells.
Cirrhosis observed in approximately 15% of individuals with alcohol use disorders.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.title: Impact of Alcohol on Cardiac Function and Hypertension
items.content: Acute ethanol consumption decreases myocardial contractility and causes peripheral vasodilation, leading to a mild decrease in blood pressure with compensatory cardiac output increase. Chronic heavy drinking is associated with hypertension and increased risk for coronary artery disease and cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Ethanol acutely decreases myocardial contractility and causes peripheral vasodilation.
Three or more drinks per day can lead to a dose-dependent increase in blood pressure, which normalizes within weeks of abstinence.
Chronic heavy drinking increases the risk for coronary artery disease sixfold due to increased low-density lipoprotein cholesterol.
Cardiomyopathy symptoms include arrhythmias, heart failure, hypocontractility, and dilation of all four heart chambers with potential mural thrombi and mitral valve regurgitation.
'Holiday heart' syndrome: atrial or ventricular arrhythmias after heavy drinking in individuals without other evidence of heart disease.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Effects of Alcohol on Reproductive Health and Fetal Development
items.content: Heavy alcohol consumption affects sexual development in adolescents and can impair reproductive function at any age. In pregnant women, ethanol exposure leads to fetal alcohol spectrum disorders, including severe conditions like fetal alcohol syndrome.
keyPoints: Heavy drinking during adolescence impacts normal sexual development and reproductive onset.
Modest ethanol doses (e.g., blood alcohol concentrations of 0.06 g/dL) can increase sexual drive but decrease erectile capacity in men.
Chronic heavy drinking may cause irreversible testicular atrophy, decreased ejaculate volume, and lower sperm count even without liver impairment.
In women, high ethanol intake can lead to amenorrhea, decreased ovarian size, infertility, and increased risk of spontaneous abortion.
Fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD) result from ethanol exposure during pregnancy, with fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS) occurring in ~5% of children born to heavy-drinking mothers.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.title: Alcoholic Myopathy and Skeletal Effects in Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: Individuals with alcohol use disorders often experience skeletal muscle weakness due to acute alcoholic myopathy, which may improve but not fully remit with abstinence. Repeated heavy drinking leads to changes in calcium metabolism, lower bone density, and decreased growth in the epiphyses, increasing fracture risk and osteonecrosis of the femoral head.
keyPoints: 1-2/3 individuals with alcohol use disorders have skeletal muscle weakness due to acute alcoholic myopathy.
Alcoholic myopathy may improve but not fully remit with abstinence.
Heavy drinking affects calcium metabolism, lowers bone density, and decreases epiphyseal growth.
Increased risk for fractures and osteonecrosis of the femoral head.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.title: Hormonal Changes in Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: Alcohol use disorders are associated with hormonal changes, including elevated cortisol levels that may persist during heavy drinking. There is inhibition of vasopressin secretion at rising blood alcohol concentrations and enhanced secretion as it falls, leading to slight overhydration. Serum thyroxine (T4) decreases modestly and reversibly, while serum triiodothyronine (T3) shows a more marked decrease. These hormonal irregularities typically resolve after one month or more of abstinence.
keyPoints: Cortisol levels increase during heavy drinking and may remain elevated.
Vasopressin secretion is inhibited at rising blood alcohol concentrations and enhanced as it falls, causing slight overhydration.
Serum thyroxine (T4) decreases modestly and reversibly.
Serum triiodothyronine (T3) shows a more marked decrease.
Hormonal irregularities usually resolve after one month or more of abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.title: Genetic Contribution to Alcohol Use Disorder Risk
items.content: Approximately 60% of the risk for alcohol use disorder is attributed to genetic factors. This includes a fourfold increased risk in children with an affected parent, even if adopted early and raised by nonalcoholics, and a higher concordance rate among identical twins compared to fraternal twins.
keyPoints: 60% of the risk for alcohol use disorder is attributed to genetic factors.
Children with an alcohol use disorder parent have a fourfold increased risk.
Higher risk in identical twins compared to fraternal twins.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.title: Low Sensitivity to Alcohol and Its Genetic Basis
items.content: A genetically influenced characteristic that increases the risk for heavy drinking is a low level of response or sensitivity to alcohol. This trait, which can be observed early in one's drinking career, involves variations in genes related to calcium and potassium channels, GABA, nicotinic, dopamine, and serotonin systems.
keyPoints: Low sensitivity to alcohol increases the risk for heavy drinking.
Genetic variations involve calcium and potassium channels, GABA, nicotinic, dopamine, and serotonin systems.
This low response predicts future heavy drinking and alcohol problems.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.title: Early Onset of Drinking as a Predictor for Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: An earlier onset of regular drinking and drunkenness, particularly in the context of conduct problems, is associated with an increased risk of developing alcohol use disorders. This pattern often leads to major life problems by late teens or early twenties.
keyPoints: Average age of first drink ~15 years for both those who develop and do not develop alcohol use disorders.
Earlier onset of regular drinking linked with higher risk for later alcohol-related diagnoses.
Major life problems from alcohol often appear in the late teens to early twenties.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: Following treatment, more than half of those with alcohol use disorder maintain a significant decrease in alcohol use or achieve full abstinence for at least one year. There is also a 20% chance of spontaneous remission without formal treatment.
keyPoints: More than half maintain decreased alcohol use or achieve abstinence post-treatment for at least a year.
At least 20% chance of spontaneous remission with long-term abstinence, even without formal treatment.
Continued heavy drinking shortens lifespan by ~10 years, with increased risk of heart disease, cancer, accidents, and suicide.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.title: Screening for Alcohol Use Disorders: Key Laboratory Tests and Diagnostic Criteria
items.content: To identify patients with alcohol use disorders, focus on specific laboratory tests that reflect heavy consumption. The combination of $\gamma$-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels greater than 35 U and carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) levels above 20 U/L or 2.6% is highly sensitive and specific for detecting alcohol use disorders. Other supportive tests include high-normal mean corpuscular volume (MCV ≥91 μm³) and serum uric acid levels exceeding 416 mol/L (7 mg/dL). Diagnosis should be based on a documented pattern of repeated problems related to alcohol, using tools like the AUDIT questionnaire as initial screening aids.
keyPoints: $\gamma$-glutamyl transferase (GGT) >35 U
Carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) >20 U/L or >2.6%
High-normal MCV ≥91 μm³
Serum uric acid >416 mol/L (7 mg/dL)
Use of AUDIT questionnaire for initial screening
Diagnosis based on repeated alcohol-related problems
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.title: Management of Acute Alcohol Intoxication: Priorities and Pharmacological Options
items.content: In cases of severe alcohol intoxication, the primary focus should be on assessing and stabilizing vital signs. Manage any respiratory depression, cardiac arrhythmias, or blood pressure instability promptly. Consider toxicology screens for other CNS depressants like benzodiazepines and opioids if polydrug use is suspected. For aggressive behavior, start with reassurance and call for help from an intervention team. If necessary, administer low doses of a short-acting benzodiazepine such as lorazepam (1–2 mg PO or IV), ensuring vital signs remain stable. Alternatively, use antipsychotic medication like olanzapine (2.5–10 mg IM) if needed.
keyPoints: Assess and stabilize vital signs
Manage respiratory depression, cardiac arrhythmias, blood pressure instability
Consider toxicology screens for other CNS depressants
Use lorazepam 1–2 mg PO or IV for aggressive behavior
Alternative: Olanzapine 2.5–10 mg IM if needed
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.title: Motivational Interviewing for Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: Motivational interviewing is a patient-centered approach used to encourage individuals with alcohol use disorders to consider the benefits and drawbacks of their drinking habits. The clinician engages empathetically, helping patients weigh options and take responsibility for change.
keyPoints: Involves discussing assets (e.g., comfort in social situations) and liabilities (e.g., health- and interpersonal-related problems).
Empathetic listening is crucial to help the patient consider changes.
Patients are reminded they have control over avoiding consequences of heavy drinking.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.title: Brief Interventions Using FRAMES for Alcohol Use Disorders
items.content: The brief intervention approach, summarized by the acronym FRAMES, provides a structured method to motivate patients with alcohol use disorders. It emphasizes feedback, responsibility, advice, options, empathy, and self-efficacy.
keyPoints: Feedback: Provide information about the patient's drinking patterns.
Responsibility: Emphasize that the patient is responsible for change.
Advice: Offer guidance rather than orders on necessary actions.
Menus: Present a range of options for consideration.
Empathy: Show understanding of the patient's feelings and thoughts.
Self-efficacy: Support the patient’s belief in their ability to make changes.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.title: Timing and Management of Alcohol Withdrawal Symptoms
items.content: Alcohol withdrawal symptoms typically begin within 5–10 hours after reducing alcohol intake, peak on days 2 or 3, and improve by days 4 or 5. However, mild symptoms can persist for 4–6 months as a protracted abstinence syndrome.
keyPoints: Withdrawal symptoms onset: 5-10 hours post-alcohol reduction
Symptom peak: Day 2 or 3
Improvement by: Day 4 or 5
Protracted abstinence syndrome duration: 4–6 months
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.title: Risk Factors and Prevention of Severe Withdrawal Complications
items.content: Approximately 2% of individuals with alcohol use disorder experience withdrawal seizures, while about 1% develop delirium tremens (DTs). Risk factors include older age, concomitant medical problems, additional drug misuse, and higher alcohol quantities. Early identification and treatment of underlying conditions, along with benzodiazepine administration, can reduce these risks.
keyPoints: Withdrawal seizure risk: ~2%
Delirium tremens (DTs) risk: ~1%
Risk factors for seizures/DTs: older age, concomitant medical problems, additional drug misuse, higher alcohol quantities
Prevention strategy: early identification and treatment of underlying conditions, benzodiazepine use
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.title: Treatment Approach for Alcohol Withdrawal
items.content: Withdrawal symptoms can be managed with depressant medications like benzodiazepines. Chlordiazepoxide or diazepam are commonly used, with initial doses of 25–50 mg and 10 mg respectively, given every 4–6 hours on the first day, tapering over 5 days. Patients with mild symptoms may be considered for outpatient detoxification.
keyPoints: Benzodiazepines: primary treatment
Chlordiazepoxide initial dose: 25-50 mg PO every 4-6 hours
Diazepam initial dose: 10 mg PO every 4-6 hours
Tapering period: over 5 days
Outpatient detoxification consideration: mild symptoms, no prior DTs or seizures
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1)
items.title: Optimizing Motivation and Coping Strategies in Alcohol Rehabilitation
items.content: The rehabilitation phase for alcohol use disorders emphasizes cognitive-behavioral approaches to enhance patient compliance. Key steps include optimizing motivation through education, recognizing the non-stereotypical profile of patients, and developing coping strategies to prevent relapse.
keyPoints: ≥50% of individuals maintain abstinence or significant reduction in alcohol intake for at least a year post-rehabilitation.
Cognitive-behavioral approaches are core components during rehabilitation.
Relapse prevention education is crucial, focusing on identifying and mitigating risky situations.
Ongoing contact with outpatient treatment staff should be maintained for at least 6 months to a year after abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Physicians in Alcohol Rehabilitation
items.content: Physicians play a critical role in the rehabilitation phase by identifying alcohol problems, diagnosing associated conditions, overseeing detoxification, and providing counseling. They also guide medication use for insomnia and anxiety during recovery.
keyPoints: Physicians should oversee detoxification and refer patients to appropriate rehabilitation programs.
For insomnia, sleep hygiene is recommended over depressant medications due to potential rebound effects.
Sedating antidepressants are not optimal; occasional use of sedating antihistamines can be considered.
Anxiety symptoms can be managed with cognitive therapy.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.title: Medications in Alcohol Rehabilitation
items.content: The efficacy of medications during the rehabilitation phase is modest and best measured alongside cognitive-behavioral approaches. Naltrexone, an opioid antagonist, shows potential benefits in reducing relapse duration.
keyPoints: Controlled trials indicate few medications with modest benefits within 6–12 months post-recovery.
Naltrexone may shorten subsequent relapses and is used orally at doses of 50–150 mg/d.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.question: Which of the following is a direct effect of even relatively low doses of alcohol?
items.explanation: Even at relatively low doses, alcohol can exacerbate most medical problems.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.question: What is the lifetime risk for repetitive serious alcohol problems in women?
items.explanation: The lifetime risk for repetitive serious alcohol problems in women is at least 10%.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.question: What does the acronym SBIRT stand for?
options: Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment
Standardized Behavioral Interventions and Recovery Training
Systematic Biopsychosocial Integration and Rehabilitation Techniques
items.explanation: SBIRT stands for Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.question: Which enzyme is primarily responsible for the metabolism of ethanol to acetaldehyde in the liver?
items.explanation: Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) is the enzyme primarily responsible for converting ethanol to acetaldehyde in the liver.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of ethanol is typically excreted directly through urine at high blood alcohol concentrations?
items.explanation: Between 2% and 10% of ethanol is excreted directly, with the higher end being around 10% at high blood alcohol concentrations.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.question: Which neurotransmitter system's activity is acutely boosted by alcohol, contributing to its antianxiety effects?
items.explanation: Alcohol acutely boosts gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) activity, particularly at GABA_A receptors.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.question: What is the effect of alcohol on gluconeogenesis in a fasting individual?
items.explanation: Alcohol decreases gluconeogenesis, which can lead to transient hypoglycemia in fasting individuals.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.question: Which blood alcohol concentration is associated with deep but disturbed sleep in individuals who have not developed tolerance?
items.explanation: Deep but disturbed sleep can be seen at 0.15 g/dL in individuals who have not developed tolerance.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.question: Which type of tolerance involves neurochemical changes that maintain relatively normal physiologic functioning despite the presence of alcohol?
items.explanation: Cellular or pharmacodynamic tolerance develops through neurochemical changes that maintain relatively normal physiologic functioning despite the presence of alcohol.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.question: What is the primary mechanism behind acquired tolerance to alcohol after repeated use?
options: Decreased dopamine activity in the ventral tegmental region
Compensatory mechanisms including metabolic and pharmacodynamic changes
Enhanced GABA release without any other changes
items.explanation: Acquired tolerance involves at least three compensatory mechanisms, including metabolic or pharmacokinetic tolerance and cellular or pharmacodynamic tolerance.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.question: What percentage of drinkers, including college students and individuals with alcohol use disorders, experience a blackout?
items.explanation: Approximately 35% of drinkers overall, including as many as 50% of drinking college students and a higher proportion of individuals with alcohol use disorders, experience a blackout.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.question: What is the effect of alcohol on sleep architecture?
options: It increases REM and deep sleep.
It has no significant impact on sleep stages.
It disrupts sleep by altering sleep stages, reducing REM and deep sleep early in the night.
It enhances muscle relaxation without affecting sleep stages.
items.explanation: Alcohol initially helps a person fall asleep but disrupts sleep throughout the rest of the night by altering sleep stages, reducing times spent in REM and deep sleep early in the night.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.question: What is a common consequence of alcohol use even at relatively low levels?
options: Impaired judgment and coordination, increasing the risk of injuries.
items.explanation: Even at relatively low alcohol levels, common consequences include impaired judgment and coordination, which increase the risk of injuries.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.question: What percentage of individuals with alcohol use disorders develop cerebellar degeneration or atrophy?
items.explanation: Approximately 1% of those with alcohol use disorders develop cerebellar degeneration or atrophy, while peripheral neuropathy occurs in ~10%.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of hemorrhagic gastritis due to alcohol use?
items.explanation: Alcohol can cause inflammation leading to hemorrhagic gastritis, and violent vomiting from alcohol use can produce a Mallory-Weiss lesion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.question: What is the estimated increase in risk for oral and esophageal cancers with four drinks per day?
items.explanation: Four drinks per day increases the risk for oral and esophageal cancers approximately threefold.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.question: Which hematologic change is commonly associated with chronic heavy drinking?
options: Hypersegmented neutrophils without folic acid deficiency
items.explanation: Chronic heavy drinking is associated with mild thrombocytopenia, which usually resolves within a week of abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.question: Which of the following is a potential acute cardiovascular effect of ethanol consumption?
options: Peripheral vasodilation with mild decrease in blood pressure
items.explanation: Ethanol acutely causes peripheral vasodilation and a mild decrease in blood pressure.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.question: What is the risk associated with chronic heavy drinking related to coronary artery disease?
options: Sixfold increased risk for coronary artery disease
No significant change in cardiovascular health
items.explanation: Chronic heavy drinkers have a sixfold increased risk for coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.question: What is 'holiday heart' syndrome?
options: A permanent increase in blood pressure after heavy drinking
Temporary atrial or ventricular arrhythmias following heavy drinking
Chronic cardiomyopathy due to alcohol use
items.explanation: 'Holiday heart' refers to temporary arrhythmias after heavy drinking in individuals without other heart disease.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.question: Which hormonal change associated with alcohol use disorders can lead to slight overhydration in affected individuals?
options: Inhibition of vasopressin secretion at rising blood alcohol concentrations
Enhanced secretion of vasopressin at falling blood alcohol concentrations
items.explanation: Enhanced secretion of vasopressin at falling blood alcohol concentrations leads to slight overhydration.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.question: What is a skeletal effect of repeated heavy drinking in individuals with alcohol use disorders?
options: Changes in calcium metabolism and lower bone density
items.explanation: Repeated heavy drinking leads to changes in calcium metabolism, lower bone density, and decreased growth in the epiphyses.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.question: Which of the following is a reversible effect of alcohol use disorders on thyroid hormones?
options: Modest and reversible decrease in serum thyroxine (T4)
items.explanation: Alcohol use disorders cause a modest and reversible decrease in serum thyroxine (T4).
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.question: What percentage of the risk for alcohol use disorder is attributed to genetic factors?
items.explanation: Approximately 60% of the risk for alcohol use disorder is attributed to genes.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.question: Which gene variation is associated with a decreased risk specifically for alcohol use disorders due to an intense skin flushing response?
items.explanation: Gene variations in ALDH, especially a mutation seen in Japanese, Chinese, and Korean individuals, are associated with decreased risk for alcohol use disorders due to an intense skin flushing response.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a gene system related to low sensitivity to alcohol?
items.explanation: Opioid receptors are not mentioned in relation to low sensitivity to alcohol; calcium and potassium channels, GABA, nicotinic, dopamine, and serotonin systems are.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.question: Which factor is associated with a higher risk of developing alcohol-related diagnoses?
options: An average age of first drink around 15 years
Later onset of regular drinking and drunkenness
Earlier onset of regular drinking and drunkenness, especially in the context of conduct problems
items.explanation: An earlier onset of regular drinking and drunkenness, particularly with conduct issues, increases the risk for later alcohol-related diagnoses.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.question: What is a common outcome for individuals with alcohol use disorders by their mid-twenties?
options: Most achieve full abstinence without treatment
They typically moderate their drinking habits
A pattern of multiple alcohol difficulties often develops
Their life span increases due to controlled drinking
items.explanation: Individuals with alcohol use disorders are likely to develop a pattern of multiple alcohol-related problems by mid-twenties.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.question: What is the average reduction in life span for individuals who continue heavy drinking?
items.explanation: Continued heavy drinking shortens the life span by an average of ~10 years.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.question: Which two blood tests have ≥60% sensitivity and specificity for heavy alcohol consumption?
options: $ \gamma $-glutamyl transferase (GGT) (>35 U) and carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) (>20 U/L or >2.6%)
Serum uric acid (>416 mol/L, or 7 mg/dL) and high-normal MCVs (≥91 μm³)
Carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) (>20 U/L or >2.6%) and serum bilirubin (>1.2 mg/dL)
Serum uric acid (>416 mol/L, or 7 mg/dL) and $ \gamma $-glutamyl transferase (GGT) (>35 U)
items.explanation: $ \gamma $-glutamyl transferase (GGT) and carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) are the tests with ≥60% sensitivity and specificity for heavy alcohol consumption.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.question: What is a first priority in treating severe intoxication?
options: Administering high doses of benzodiazepines immediately
Assessing vital signs and managing respiratory depression, cardiac arrhythmias, and blood pressure instability
Conducting a face-to-face interview to diagnose alcohol use disorder
Using standardized questionnaires like the AUDIT for immediate diagnosis
items.explanation: The first priority in treating severe intoxication is assessing vital signs and managing respiratory depression, cardiac arrhythmias, and blood pressure instability.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.question: Which medication can be used to manage aggressive behavior in acute intoxication?
options: Olanzapine 2.5–10 mg IM repeated at 2 and 6 h, if needed
items.explanation: Olanzapine can be used to manage aggressive behavior in acute intoxication, with a dose of 2.5–10 mg IM repeated at 2 and 6 h if needed.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.question: Which acronym summarizes the process of brief intervention for alcohol use disorder?
items.explanation: The FRAMES acronym is used to summarize the components of a brief intervention.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.question: What does 'S' stand for in the SBIRT approach?
items.explanation: 'S' stands for Screening, which is part of the SBIRT approach.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.question: What aspect does motivational interviewing emphasize in helping patients with alcohol use disorder?
items.explanation: Motivational interviewing emphasizes encouraging self-reflection and taking responsibility for changes.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.question: Which symptom is NOT typically associated with alcohol withdrawal?
items.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal symptoms include autonomic nervous system overactivity, which typically results in an increase in pulse rate.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.question: What is the peak time for alcohol withdrawal symptoms?
options: Within 1-2 hours of decreasing ethanol intake
On day 4 or 5 after decreasing ethanol intake
On day 2 or 3 after decreasing ethanol intake
items.explanation: Withdrawal symptoms usually peak on day 2 or 3 after the decrease in alcohol intake.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.question: Which medication is recommended for controlling alcohol withdrawal seizures?
items.explanation: Benzodiazepines are effective in controlling alcohol withdrawal seizures, and anticonvulsants like phenytoin or gabapentin have not shown more efficacy.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.question: Which of the following is a recommended initial step for managing potential alcohol withdrawal?
options: Administering high doses of benzodiazepines immediately
Providing IV fluids to all patients
items.explanation: A thorough physical examination is the first step in managing potential alcohol withdrawal.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.question: What is a key step in optimizing motivation toward abstinence during the rehabilitation phase for individuals with alcohol use disorders?
options: Providing education about alcohol use disorders to patients and their significant others
Encouraging immediate employment or vocational counseling
Administering opioid antagonists like naltrexone immediately upon admission
Ensuring complete abstinence without any relapse prevention strategies
items.explanation: Education of patients and their significant others about alcohol use disorders is crucial for optimizing motivation toward abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended approach for addressing insomnia in patients recovering from alcohol use disorders?
options: Avoiding any form of sleep intervention
items.explanation: 'Sleep hygiene' practices are recommended for managing insomnia in patients recovering from alcohol use disorders.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.question: Which medication has shown modest benefits in the first 6–12 months of recovery from an alcohol use disorder?
items.explanation: The opioid antagonist naltrexone has shown modest benefits in the first 6–12 months of recovery from an alcohol use disorder.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the role of self-help groups like Alcoholics Anonymous in the rehabilitation phase?
options: They provide medication for alcohol use disorders
They assist patients in developing a sober peer group and learning stress management without drinking
items.explanation: Self-help groups like Alcoholics Anonymous help patients develop a sober peer group and learn how to manage life's stresses while remaining sober.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.statement: Drinking more than three standard drinks per day decreases the risk for cancer.
items.explanation: Drinking more than three standard drinks per day enhances the risk for cancer.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders
items.statement: The yearly costs of alcohol disorders in the U.S. exceed $249 billion.
items.explanation: U.S. yearly costs for these disorders indeed exceed $249 billion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.statement: A standard drink contains approximately 10 grams of ethanol.
items.explanation: A standard drink is defined as containing about 10-12 grams of ethanol.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.statement: Alcohol increases the storage of vitamins in the liver.
items.explanation: Alcohol interferes with the absorption and decreases the storage of vitamins in the liver.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.statement: Behavioral, psychomotor, and cognitive changes are seen at blood alcohol levels of 0.15 g/dL.
items.explanation: These changes are observed at lower levels, specifically 0.02–0.04 g/dL.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.statement: Protracted withdrawal symptoms can last up to 4–6 months.
items.explanation: Symptoms such as disturbed sleep and anxiety may persist for 4–6 months during protracted withdrawal.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.statement: Alcohol-induced psychiatric symptoms that are only observed during intense intoxication or withdrawal syndromes typically disappear within days to weeks of abstinence.
items.explanation: These alcohol-induced psychiatric symptoms usually resolve within days to weeks after achieving abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.statement: Brain ventricular enlargement and widened cortical sulci due to long-term alcohol use disorders are irreversible.
items.explanation: These changes are usually reversible if abstinence is maintained.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.statement: Alcohol use disorders are associated with an increased rate of hepatitis C.
items.explanation: Individuals with alcohol use disorders have an elevated rate of hepatitis C.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.statement: Heavy drinking leads to irreversible changes in liver cells.
items.explanation: In healthy individuals, the changes caused by alcohol are reversible, but repeated exposure can lead to severe and potentially irreversible damage.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.statement: Heavy drinking can lead to a dose-dependent increase in blood pressure that normalizes within weeks of abstinence.
items.explanation: Blood pressure increases with heavy drinking and returns to normal after cessation.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.statement: Fetal alcohol syndrome is observed in approximately 50% of children born to heavy-drinking mothers.
items.explanation: Fetal alcohol syndrome is seen in ~5% of such cases.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.statement: The lifetime risk for an alcohol use disorder is higher among physicians compared to the general population.
items.explanation: In Western countries, the lifetime risk of alcohol use disorder among physicians is similar to that of the general population.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS
items.statement: Alcohol-related skeletal muscle weakness may not fully remit with abstinence.
items.explanation: Acute alcoholic myopathy can cause skeletal muscle weakness that might not fully improve even with abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ☐ OTHER EFFECTS | ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.statement: Gene variations related to impulsivity, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder contribute to the risk for all substance use disorders.
items.explanation: Genes relating to a high risk for all substance use disorders operate through traits such as impulsivity, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: GENETICS
items.statement: The low response per drink to alcohol is associated with future problems with drugs other than alcohol.
items.explanation: A low response per drink predicts future heavy drinking and alcohol-related problems, but not problems with drugs other than alcohol.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · GENETICS

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.statement: More than half of those with alcohol use disorder maintain a marked decrease in alcohol use or achieve full abstinence for at least a year following treatment.
items.explanation: Following treatment, over half of individuals with alcohol use disorders show significant reduction in use or achieve abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT
items.statement: There is less than a 10% chance of spontaneous remission without formal treatment.
items.explanation: Even without formal treatment, there is at least a 20% chance of spontaneous remission.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · NATURAL HISTORY | IDENTIFICATION AND TREATMENT

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.statement: The values for serologic markers of heavy alcohol consumption return to normal within several weeks of abstinence.
items.explanation: Serologic markers like GGT and CDT are likely to return toward normal within several weeks of abstinence.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.statement: The diagnostic criteria for alcohol use disorder in DSM-IV and DSM-5 are significantly different.
items.explanation: The diagnostic criteria in DSM-IV and DSM-5 are very similar.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.statement: Brief interventions can be completed in a single session.
items.explanation: Multiple meetings are often required to explore the problem and possible options.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.statement: Motivational interviewing involves listening empathetically to patients' responses.
items.explanation: Empathetic listening is a key component of motivational interviewing.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.statement: Antipsychotic medications are recommended for treating alcohol withdrawal symptoms.
items.explanation: Antipsychotic medications are not recommended as they may increase the risk of seizures.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.statement: The mortality rate associated with delirium tremens can be as high as 5%.
items.explanation: Delirium tremens is a medical emergency with an estimated mortality rate of up to 5%.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.statement: Depressant sleep medications are the optimal approach for insomnia in patients recovering from alcohol use disorders.
items.explanation: Depressant sleep medications are not recommended due to risks of rebound insomnia and potential dependence.

items.subtopic: HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)
items.statement: The physician's role includes overseeing detoxification and referring patients to appropriate rehabilitation programs.
items.explanation: Physicians play a crucial role in the management of alcohol use disorders, including overseeing detoxification and referrals.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 1) | HELPING INDIVIDUALS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS TO STOP OR SIGNIFICANTLY DECREASE DRINKING: THE REHABILITATION PHASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.assertion: Acute alcohol consumption leads to an increase in GABA receptor density.
items.reason: Continued use of alcohol increases the density of GABA_A receptors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)
items.assertion: Alcohol withdrawal is characterized by increased GABA-related activity.
items.reason: Acute alcohol consumption inhibits postsynaptic NMDA receptors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because alcohol withdrawal decreases GABA-related activity, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL
items.assertion: Metabolic or pharmacokinetic tolerance can develop after 1–2 weeks of daily drinking.
items.reason: This type of tolerance involves up to a 30% increase in the rate of hepatic ethanol metabolism.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · PHARMACOLOGY AND NUTRITIONAL IMPACT OF ETHANOL (part 2) | BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS, TOLERANCE, AND WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.assertion: Alcohol can lead to peripheral neuropathy in individuals with alcohol use disorders.
items.reason: Peripheral neuropathy occurs due to chronic high doses of alcohol, affecting ~10% of these individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.assertion: Alcohol use disorders can lead to irreversible cognitive changes.
items.reason: There is a single 'alcoholic dementia' syndrome that describes these changes.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; there is no single 'alcoholic dementia' syndrome.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE EFFECTS OF ETHANOL ON ORGAN SYSTEMS | NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM
items.assertion: Alcohol use increases the risk of various cancers.
items.reason: This is due to alcohol's cancer-promoting effects and its interference with immune homeostasis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why alcohol use increases cancer risk.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · THE GASTROINTESTINAL SYSTEM | CANCER | HEMATOPOIETIC SYSTEM

items.subtopic: CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT
items.assertion: Fetal alcohol syndrome can include microcephaly with intellectual impairment.
items.reason: Ethanol and acetaldehyde rapidly transfer through the placenta during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why fetal alcohol syndrome includes these features.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM | GENITOURINARY SYSTEM CHANGES, SEXUAL FUNCTIONING, AND FETAL DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION
items.assertion: Standardized questionnaires like the AUDIT can be used for a meaningful diagnosis of alcohol use disorder.
items.reason: These are only screening tools, and a face-to-face interview is still required for a meaningful diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because standardized questionnaires alone cannot provide a meaningful diagnosis; they require confirmation through a face-to-face interview.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · IDENTIFICATION OF PATIENTS WITH ALCOHOL USE DISORDERS | ACUTE INTOXICATION

items.subtopic: INTERVENTION
items.assertion: The SBIRT approach includes treatment or referral to treatment.
items.reason: SBIRT stands for Screening, Brief Interventions, and Treatment or Referral to Treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · INTERVENTION

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.assertion: Short-half-life benzodiazepines are preferred for patients with significant brain damage.
items.reason: They must be given every 4 hours to avoid abrupt blood-level fluctuations that may increase the risk of seizures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why short-half-life benzodiazepines are considered for patients with significant brain damage.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.subtopic: ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL
items.assertion: Alcohol withdrawal symptoms can be managed by administering any CNS depressant.
items.reason: The symptoms reflect the acute decrease in blood levels of alcohol, a CNS depressant.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CNS depressants manage withdrawal symptoms.
items.reference: Alcohol and Alcohol Use Disorders · ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL

items.reason: These approaches help patients comply with treatment goals and improve health and quality of life.

items.assertion: The efficacy of medications in treating alcohol use disorders is easily measurable.
items.reason: Medications have large effect sizes that are straightforward to document.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are false; measuring medication efficacy is challenging due to modest effect sizes.
